content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Richard M. Flores 
Deputy County Counsel 
Mendocino County Courthouse 
Ukiah, CA 
95482 
Dear Mr. Flores: 
March 5, 1987 
Re: 
Your Request for Assistance 
Our File No. I-87-043 
You have written requesting advice regarding a possible 
conflict of interest on the part of an unnamed county 
employee. 
Since your request does not involve a specific 
pending decision, we are treating it as a request for informal 
assistance . .!! 
You have specifically asked about potential conflicts of 
interest under Government Code section 1126. 
That section 
falls outside the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").Y Consequently, we are unable to advise you with 
regard to that issue. 
We suggest that you contact the Attorney 
General for an interpretation of the applicability of that 
section to your circumstances. 
with respect to the provisions of the Act, you have sought 
general guidance regarding potential conflicts of interest for 
a county "plan checker" who is also privately employed in the 
construction industry. 
We have not been provided with detailed 
information regarding the nature and circumstances of this 
employment; however, the Act requires that any public official 
disqualify himself from making, participating in making, or 
using his official position to influence the making of any 
governmental decision in which he has a financial interest . 
.!! 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation l8329(c) (3).) 
Y 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California 
Administrative Code section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • 
(916)322~5660 


Richard M. Flores 
February 27, 1987 
page 2 
(Section 87100.) 
An official has a financial interest in a 
decision if it will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the official, a member of the official's 
immediate family or on: 
... (c) 
Any source of income, other than gifts 
and other than loans by a commercial lending 
institution in the regular course of business on terms 
available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) 
or more in value provided to, received by or promised 
to the public official within 12 months prior to the 
time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management .... 
Obviously, the private employer is a source of income to the 
person as well as a business entity. 
(Section 87103(c) and 
(d).) 
Hence, disqualification will be required with respect to 
decisions which will affect the employer. 
(See Regulation 
18702.1, copy enclosed.) 
The employer's plans should be 
referred to another county employee for checking, without any 
input or supervision from the employee in question. 
We also 
enclose a copy of the Commission's Maloney Opinion, 3 FPPC Ops. 
(No. 76-082, Aug. 18, 1977), which involved a part-time county 
surveyor who also practiced his profession privately within the 
same county. 
Since we have not been provided with detailed information 
regarding the specifics of his work in the private sector, it 
is not possible to provide you with more detailed advice. 
When 
more facts are known, you may wish to contact us again and 
provide us with the name and other facts material to our more 
complete review and advice. 
For questions regarding this 
letter, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths

H. PETER KLEIN 
COUNTY COUNSEL 
OFFICE OF THE 
COUNTY COUNSEL 
COURTHOUSE 
UKIAH, CALIFORNIA 95482 
Jan <ti°11 i:Y-44J.62 , 1 9 8 7 
Diane Griffiths, General Counsel 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K street Mall 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
RONALD R. BALL 
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL 
YVES A. HEBERT 
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL 
JOSEPH DURANTE 
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL 
RE: 
REQUEST FOR OPINION -
POTENTIALLY CONFLICTING EMPLOYMENT 
Dear Ms. Griffiths: 
The County of Mendocino finds itself in a situation where 
there might be a conflict of interest in the private employment 
of one of its employees (pursuant to Government Code Section 
1126). 
The County requests an opinion from the Fair Political 
Practices Commission regarding this matter. 
The question 
presented is as follows: 
Question: 
Is a County employee's position as "plan checker" 
for the Planning and Building Services Department incompatible 
and/or in conflict with his private employment as a building 
contractor? 
The Planning and Building Services Department is responsible 
for inspection of residential and commercial construction plans. 
It enforces compliance with uniform codes required by state and 
local ordinances and laws. 
It does actual visual 
inspections/approvals/reviews of on-site construction. 
It 
approves the appropriate building permits. 
All of the employee's work as a building contractor 
(applicable permits, building inspections, etc.) is subject to 
review/approval by the Planning and Building Services Department. 
The affected employee of the County has agreed to hold in 
abeyance any work that he might have done as a private building 
contractor until an opinion has been received from the Fair 
Political Practices Commission. 
A copy of the job description 
for a "plan checker" is attached hereto for your review. 
Thank you for your efforts 
RMF/cf

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Richard M. Flores 
Deputy County Counsel 
County of Mendocino 
Courthouse 
Ukiah, CA 
95482 
Dear Mr. Flores: 
February 5, 1987 
Re: 
87-043 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on February 2, 1987 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Robert E. Leidigh, an attorney 
in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days. 
You also should be aware that your 
letter and our response are public records which may be 
disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for 
disclosure.
